front: What is the primary data model used by RDBMS and Hadoop?
back: RDBMS uses a structured, schema-on-write data model (tables with rows and columns). Hadoop uses an unstructured or semi-structured, schema-on-read data model, storing data in its raw format (e.g., text, JSON, logs).

front: How do RDBMS and Hadoop differ in their approach to scalability?
back: RDBMS scales vertically (scale-up) by adding more power (CPU, RAM) to a single server. Hadoop scales horizontally (scale-out) by adding more commodity nodes to a distributed cluster.

front: What is the key architectural difference regarding data processing?
back: RDBMS follows a centralized architecture where data is moved to the database server for processing. Hadoop follows a distributed processing model (MapReduce) where the processing logic is moved to the nodes where the data resides.

front: For which type of data workloads is each system best suited?
back: RDBMS is optimized for structured data and transactional, low-latency OLTP workloads. Hadoop is optimized for unstructured/semi-structured data and high-latency, batch-oriented analytical processing (OLAP) on massive datasets.

front: Contrast the cost structure of an RDBMS versus Hadoop.
back: RDBMS typically involves high licensing costs for proprietary software and expensive hardware. Hadoop is built on open-source software and is designed to run on low-cost commodity hardware, leading to a lower total cost of ownership for large-scale storage.
